text: By the end of this section, you'll understand the three sources of chemical hazards in your bakery, why the food-grade lubricant distinction is critical, and how to build an allergen program that's practical rather than comprehensive and ignored. Chemical hazards come from three sources, each requiring a different control strategy. First, cleaning chemical residues. Every time your crew washes down equipment, they apply detergent and sanitizer to surfaces that will later contact bread. If the rinse is inadequate, concentration too high, or parts reassembled while still wet with residual solution, chemicals transfer to product. The risk is highest in cop operations. Dough hooks, depositor nozzles, and mixer paddles go through a wash cycle. If rinse water isn't fresh or the operator rushes, you've introduced chemical contamination. Control this with clear rinse procedures and a post-rinse inspection. Some chemicals, particularly chlorinated sanitizers and parasitic acid, leave a detectable odor at concerning concentrations. Train your team to recognize those odors during reassembly. Second, lubricant contamination. You have dozens of lubrication points, oven chains, conveyor roller bearings, divider mechanisms, molder adjustments, slicer blades. Any can result in lubricant migrating onto product contact surfaces if seals fail or maintenance uses the wrong product. Food-grade lubricants are H1 rated under NSF classification, formulated for incidental food contact. If H1 lubricant drips onto a contact surface, it's an operational problem. If non-food-grade lubricant contaminates product, it's a chemical hazard requiring product withdrawal. The practical reality, maintenance teams sometimes have both in the workshop. If the wrong one goes on the oven chain, the error might not be discovered until a customer complaint. The control is simple. Only food-grade lubricants stored and available in production areas. Non-food-grade for forklifts and building services stays in a separate area, clearly labeled, never brought into production. Third, pest control chemicals. Rodenticides and insecticides are toxic by design. Bait stations must be tamper resistant and positioned where contamination of food materials isn't possible. Insect units should attract insects away from production lines, not above them. Fumigation of silos requires defined re-entry periods and air testing before production resumes. Now allergens. Let's be practical. In a standard bread bakery, your allergen reality is specific and manageable. Wheat and gluten are your primary ingredient. They're in every product, on every surface, in the air. Wheat is omnipresent, not typically a changeover challenge, but it must be declared accurately. Soy is the most commonly missed allergen. It's in many bread improvers as soya flour or soya lecithin. When your technical team writes an allergen matrix, improvers get overlooked because people think of them as processing aids. Check your specifications. If they contain soy derivatives, every product using that improver contains soy. Dairy enters through milk powder, butter, and buttermilk powder in enriched doughs and specialty breads. Eggs through liquid egg, egg powder, or egg wash for certain lines. Here's my core principle. Don't build protocols for allergens you don't have. If your bakery doesn't use tree nuts, peanuts, or sesame, skip those protocols. Building an impressive program covering 14 allergens when you handle four creates unnecessary complexity, dilutes focus, and makes the program harder to follow. A focused program covering your actual four or five allergens with clear changeover procedures, validated cleaning, and analytical verification is far more effective than a comprehensive program that nobody fully implements. Your program needs an accurate allergen matrix showing which allergens are in which products on which lines. Product sequencing rules to minimize changeovers, documented changeover procedures validated with analytical testing, clear labeling with independent verification, and regular review as products, ingredients, and suppliers change. Recap. Chemical hazards come from three sources. Cleaning residues controlled through proper rinsing, lubricant contamination controlled by keeping only food grade H1 lubricants in production, and pest chemicals controlled through placement and documentation. For allergens, know your actuals. Wheat everywhere, soy in improvers, dairy in milk powder, eggs in certain lines. Don't build protocols for what you don't handle. A practical program your team follows beats a comprehensive one that lives in a binder.
